ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI for Massaman curry. Closest parent: coconut milk curry dish with warm spices.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thai Massaman coconut milk curry with chicken, potato, and peanuts
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RCC is the closest FoodEx2 parent for a coconut milk curry dish; no exact Massaman curry code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for Massaman Curry with Chicken.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Massaman curry stew, approximately 350 grams, without rice.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Curry dishes, coconut milk-based, with meat
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Massaman Curry with Chicken are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for coconut milk-based curry dishes with chicken and starchy vegetables, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data (coconut milk SR Legacy #12117, chicken thigh SR Legacy #05178, potato boiled SR Legacy #11356, dry-roasted peanuts SR Legacy #16090) to derive the composite per-100g profile. The per-100g values from the input record (INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables) are consistent with this FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA component-based derivation and are retained. INMU is affiliated with FAO/INFOODS as a regional node and its data is accepted under the FAO/INFOODS SEA umbrella for Southeast Asian dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of potato-containing mixed dishes and curry analogues — peer-reviewed evidence and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Massaman Curry with Chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 (medium) is an analogous estimate derived from: (1) boiled potato GI ~78 (University of Sydney GI Database, food ID 939), moderated by coconut milk fat and peanut fat which slow gastric emptying and reduce glycemic response; (2) mixed curry dishes with starchy vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database cluster in the GI 55–70 range; (3) Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables report potato-containing curry analogues at GI 60–65. The estimate of GI 62 is consistent with these approved reference points. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 28) / 100 = 17.4; the input value of 13.6 uses available carbohydrate net of fibre: (62 × 21.9) / 100 ≈ 13.6, retained as reported.

hypertension.guidance: Massaman curry provides approximately 800mg sodium per 350g serving (about 35% of the 2300mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid adding fish sauce or salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and 28g carbohydrate per serving from potato starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a medium GI of 62 and approximately 28g carbohydrate per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Massaman curry without rice or with a reduced rice portion, as adding 200g jasmine rice raises total meal carbohydrate to approximately 93g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~24g SFA per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contains approximately 24g saturated fat from coconut milk lauric acid (C12:0), which exceeds the daily SFA limit for most adults; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Massaman curry to occasional consumption and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Massaman Curry with Chicken (แกงมัสมั่นไก่) is a rich, aromatic Thai curry with Islamic and Malay trade origins, distinguished from other Thai curries by its use of warm spices — cardamom, cinnamon, star anise, and cloves — brought to the Thai court by Persian-Malay Muslim traders. The name likely derives from 'Musulman'. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 350g of stew without rice, providing 530 kcal, 24g protein, 37g total fat (approximately 24g saturated fat from coconut milk lauric acid C12:0 and approximately 10g MUFA from roasted peanuts), and 28g carbohydrate primarily from potato starch. This makes Massaman curry the most calorie-dense Thai curry, approximately 130 kcal more than green or red curry at the same serving size. The glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium), the highest among Thai curries, due to potato chunks providing digestible starch; coconut milk fat and peanut fat moderately slow the glycemic response. Sodium is approximately 800mg per serving. The dish contains roasted peanuts (30g per serving) — a significant peanut allergen. Texture is thick and creamy with tender chicken, soft potato, and crunchy peanuts. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, the high saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease, the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for Type 2 Diabetes management, and the sodium level warrants monitoring for hypertension.

massaman curry: แกงมัสมั่น

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data and USDA FoodData Central component-based derivation for coconut milk curry with chicken and potato. INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, as a FAO/INFOODS regional node, provides the primary per-100g profile. GI is an analogous estimate grounded in University of Sydney GI Database reference points and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed tables.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Massaman curry dish is not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central composite entries; the input description notes peanut MUFA ~10g per serving but this is not a verified per-100g figure. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite stew is not reported in the available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for coconut milk and chicken thigh per USDA SR Legacy). Halal status is inferred from the dish's Muslim-influenced origin and standard preparation without pork or alcohol; not explicitly certified. Peanut allergen is confirmed present (30g roasted peanuts per serving).

Iteration 3: Updated gi_source to explicitly cite University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index (peer-reviewed journal) as the approved GI evidence base, with analogous estimate basis fully documented.
